news_summary: Breakthroughs in AI solving longstanding math problems, recognition of emerging mathematical talent, advances in laser diode technology, and new insights into bee brain function highlight significant progress in science and technology today.
news_summary_fr: Les percées réalisées dans le domaine de l'intelligence artificielle, qui permettent de résoudre des problèmes mathématiques de longue date, la mise en avant de nouveaux talents mathématiques, les avancées en matière de technologie des diodes laser et les nouvelles découvertes sur le fonctionnement du cerveau des abeilles illustrent les progrès significatifs réalisés aujourd'hui dans les domaines de la science et de la technologie.
news_summary_es: Los avances en inteligencia artificial que resuelven problemas matemáticos de larga data, el reconocimiento de nuevos talentos matemáticos, los avances en la tecnología de diodos láser y los nuevos conocimientos sobre el funcionamiento del cerebro de las abejas ponen de relieve los importantes progresos que se están logrando hoy en día en ciencia y tecnología.

articles.title: Extremely basic AI prompt cracks decades-old maths problem
articles.summary: For the second time in a week, a long-standing conjecture has been disproved by artificial intelligence, highlighting the advanced mathematical capabilities of ChatGPT and its ilk
articles.content:
For the second time in a week, a long-standing conjecture has been disproved by artificial intelligence, highlighting the advanced mathematical capabilities of ChatGPT and its ilk

A graph showing a counterexample to the Dinitz-Garg-Goemans conjecture Dmitry Rybin

A long-standing mathematical conundrum has been solved by ChatGPT in a few hours, with only a few simple prompts.

The Dinitz-Garg-Goemans conjecture is a 30-year-old question in graph theory, but a counterexample posted on X by Dmitry Rybin, co-founder at AI start-up Autokernel, has shown that it is false.

Rybin entered just four prompts into ChatGPT 5.6 Pro: an initial one instructing the AI to “do a breakthrough and find a structured counterexample”, followed by three others simply urging it to continue searching. All four prompts added up to fewer than 60 words, and the AI took a total of 5.5 hours to crack the problem.

Advertisement

Graph theory is the study of networks made up of nodes, or vertices. The Dinitz-Garg-Goemans conjecture can be thought of as a logistical challenge: imagine shipments from a warehouse to multiple locations can be split into much smaller deliveries that can be sent on different routes. The conjecture states that this scenario can be converted into another where shipments cannot be split, and that the total cost of shipping will not increase.

Rybin didn’t respond to a request for comment, but said on X: “I know counterexamples to old conjectures are becoming a meme at this point. But I really cared about this problem and spent many weeks thinking about it.”

Free newsletter Sign up to The Daily Sign up to newsletter

Chris Bowman-Scargill at the University of York, UK, says there’s a running joke in mathematics that every conjecture in graph theory is false, just as the Dinitz-Garg-Goemans conjecture has now been proved to be.

“In fields like number theory or algebra, patterns that hold for small rank [simple situations] often hold for a long time,” says Bownman-Scargill. “Whereas in graph theory, structural behaviour can shift dramatically once you add just one or two vertices… which is how these conjectures continue to be made. You can see how people miss these things.”

AI has made rapid advances in mathematics in recent months. In May, an OpenAI model cracked a decades-old conjecture by Paul Erdős, causing a stir in mathematical circles. Earlier this week, an AI found a counterexample to the Jacobian conjecture, which had stood for nearly a century. Today, other AI users claim to have solved a Graffiti conjecture and a second graph theory problem. A website has even sprung up to catalogue the AI findings and list them by the model that was used.

Abhishek Saha at Queen Mary University of London says current AI models seem particularly well-suited to problems like the Dinitz-Garg-Goemans conjecture, but there are limits to what is currently possible – and the problems solved by AI so far are of limited complexity.

“AI is really good, and at least at some mathematical tasks, already superhuman,” says Saha. “There is a fair bit of low-hanging fruit out there. Some conjectures can now be proved or disproved by AI with very little human input; the main challenge is simply pointing the system in the right direction. On the other hand, I don’t think AI is yet at a place where it can build the theory needed to prove some of the deepest open conjectures people care about.”

But there are signs that AI is here to stay and will become a vital tool for mathematicians. Alexander Yong at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign says AI’s growing role in mathematics will empower researchers to discard dead ends and instead push in promising directions.

“Counterexamples to old conjectures are never quite as impressive as finding a sequence of interlocking arguments that constitute a proof,” says Yong. “However, I’d expect that AI will soon prove many conjectures by a combination of their inherent superhuman energy in knowing the literature and trying many things at a prompt. Those conjectures that survive AI scrutiny will be the genuine goals for human innovation.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough where AI, specifically ChatGPT, disproved a decades-old mathematical conjecture, demonstrating advanced AI capabilities with broad implications for mathematics and scientific research. This represents a meaningful technological advancement with potential to accelerate discovery and problem-solving in complex fields.
articles.personality_title: AI solves 30-year-old math problem with simple prompts
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For decades, mathematicians have studied the Dinitz-Garg-Goemans conjecture, a problem about how shipments can be split and routed efficiently in networks. This problem is part of graph theory, which looks at connections between points or nodes.

**What happened** – Dmitry Rybin, co-founder of an AI company, used ChatGPT 5.6 Pro to find a counterexample that disproves this 30-year-old conjecture. He gave the AI just four short instructions, totaling less than 60 words, and after about 5.5 hours, the AI found a solution showing the conjecture is false.

**Impact** – This is one of several recent cases where AI quickly solved or disproved long-standing math problems. It shows that AI can handle complex reasoning tasks and could help mathematicians avoid dead ends by testing ideas faster than before. However, experts say AI still cannot create deep new theories on its own.

**What's next step** – Researchers expect AI to become a common helper in math, speeding up discoveries and focusing human efforts on the hardest problems. AI might soon prove many conjectures by combining knowledge and trying many approaches quickly.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using a few simple instructions, AI has disproved a decades-old math problem, marking a new tool for speeding up mathematical discovery.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’IA résout un problème mathématique vieux de 30 ans avec des instructions simples
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis des décennies, les mathématiciens étudient la conjecture de Dinitz-Garg-Goemans, un problème sur la manière de diviser et d’acheminer efficacement des livraisons dans des réseaux. Ce problème fait partie de la théorie des graphes, qui analyse les connexions entre des points.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Dmitry Rybin, cofondateur d’une entreprise d’IA, a utilisé ChatGPT 5.6 Pro pour trouver un contre-exemple qui réfute cette conjecture vieille de 30 ans. Il a donné à l’IA seulement quatre courtes instructions, totalisant moins de 60 mots, et après environ 5,5 heures, l’IA a trouvé une solution montrant que la conjecture est fausse.

**Impact** – C’est l’un des nombreux cas récents où l’IA a rapidement résolu ou réfuté des problèmes mathématiques anciens. Cela montre que l’IA peut gérer des tâches complexes de raisonnement et aider les mathématiciens à éviter les impasses en testant les idées plus rapidement. Toutefois, les experts disent que l’IA ne peut pas encore créer de nouvelles théories profondes seule.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs pensent que l’IA deviendra un assistant courant en mathématiques, accélérant les découvertes et permettant aux humains de se concentrer sur les problèmes les plus difficiles. L’IA pourrait bientôt démontrer de nombreuses conjectures en combinant connaissances et essais rapides.

**Phrase clé** – Avec quelques instructions simples, l’IA a réfuté un problème mathématique vieux de plusieurs décennies, ouvrant la voie à une nouvelle aide pour accélérer la découverte mathématique.
articles.personality_title_es: La IA resuelve un problema matemático de 30 años con instrucciones simples
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante décadas, los matemáticos han estudiado la conjetura de Dinitz-Garg-Goemans, un problema sobre cómo dividir y enviar eficientemente entregas en redes. Este problema pertenece a la teoría de grafos, que analiza las conexiones entre puntos o nodos.

**Qué pasó** – Dmitry Rybin, cofundador de una empresa de IA, usó ChatGPT 5.6 Pro para encontrar un contraejemplo que demuestra que esta conjetura de 30 años es falsa. Le dio a la IA solo cuatro instrucciones cortas, con menos de 60 palabras en total, y después de unas 5.5 horas, la IA encontró la solución.

**Impacto** – Este es uno de varios casos recientes donde la IA resolvió o refutó rápidamente problemas matemáticos antiguos. Esto muestra que la IA puede manejar tareas complejas de razonamiento y ayudar a los matemáticos a evitar caminos sin salida probando ideas más rápido. Sin embargo, los expertos dicen que la IA todavía no puede crear nuevas teorías profundas por sí sola.

**Próximo paso** – Se espera que la IA se convierta en una herramienta común en matemáticas, acelerando descubrimientos y permitiendo que los humanos se concentren en los problemas más difíciles. La IA podría pronto demostrar muchas conjeturas combinando conocimiento y pruebas rápidas.

**Frase clave** – Con unas pocas instrucciones simples, la IA ha refutado un problema matemático de décadas, marcando una nueva herramienta para acelerar el descubrimiento matemático.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate network of interconnected nodes and edges morphs into a glowing, open lock symbolizing a broken mathematical barrier, with a gentle, warm light radiating from the center against a softly textured background of muted earth tones.

articles.title: Rising stars of mathematics awarded prestigious 2026 Fields Medal
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 23 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02169-1Two of the four are the first Chinese-born winners of the prize since 1982.
articles.content:
The four Fields Medal recipients are, from left to right, Hong Wang, Jacob Tsimerman, John Pardon and Yu Deng.Credit: Simons Foundation

Mathematicians Yu Deng, John Pardon, Jacob Tsimerman and Hong Wang have won the 2026 Fields Medal — one of the most coveted awards in their discipline. Their names were revealed at the International Congress of Mathematicians (ICM) today in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.

The four winners represent a range of subfields of maths, from number theory to mathematical physics. They had all been rumoured as favourites to win the medal, which is awarded every four years to up to four mathematicians under the age of 40.

The winners all work in North America, but two — Deng and Wang — were born and raised in China. They are only the second and third Chinese nationals to have earned a Fields Medal: Shing-Tung Yau, who is now at Tsinghua University in Beijing, won his in 1982, before either Deng or Wang were born. Wang is also only the third woman to win in the award’s 90-year history, after the late Maryam Mirzakhani in 2014 and Maryna Viazovska in 2022.

Irreversible fluids

Deng, who is 37 and grew up in Shenzhen, did his PhD at Princeton University in New Jersey. He is now at the University of Chicago in Illinois, where he specializes in differential equations, which are often used to describe physical phenomena. He says that hearing he had won the medal made him “really happy, not just for myself, but for the field I’m representing”.

Deng’s most celebrated achievement was a breakthrough on one of the problems posed by German mathematician David Hilbert in a historic talk at the ICM in 1900: he challenged mathematicians to reconcile the behaviour and smooth appearance of fluids with the idea (still not broadly accepted at that time) that they were made of multitudes of atoms or molecules.

Together with two collaborators1, Deng found a rigorous proof that the microscopic jostling of many constituent particles — acting like tiny billiard balls continuously bouncing off one another — produces as a necessary consequence a differential equation formulated in the late 1800s to describe fluids.

‘Mathematics is an unknown land’: meet Fields Medal winner Maryna Viazovska

This helped to reconcile an apparent conundrum. Microscopic physics works just as well when time is reversed — meaning that it might be impossible to tell if a movie of two molecules bouncing off each other is being played forwards or backwards. But when many molecules form a fluid, they have an unavoidably irreversible behaviour: if you mix a cold gas with a hot one, say, the mixture will never spontaneously separate back into hot and cold.

String contact

Pardon, who is at Stony Brook University on Long Island, New York, was born in Chapel Hill, North Carolina, and is also 37. He made his first original contribution to mathematics — on a deceptively simple problem about the geometry of loops on a flat surface2 — during secondary school. He went on to publish several research papers during his undergraduate degree at Princeton, where he also studied Chinese and performed as a cellist in a university orchestra.

During his PhD at Stanford University in California, Pardon partially solved another of Hilbert’s problems3, but mostly switched to symplectic and contact geometry, fields that arose from the mathematical description of physical systems, such as the motion of planets. Symplectic ‘spaces’ always have an even number of dimensions, and contact spaces are their odd-dimensional counterparts that often sit inside a symplectic space.

Ukrainian mathematician becomes second woman to win prestigious Fields Medal

Pardon’s PhD thesis was a technical tour-de-force in which he developed techniques for telling two contact spaces apart. Later, he applied those tools to cracking major problems in symplectic and contact geometry, including some that arose from string-theory physics4 — a speculative framework to interpret all elementary particles and fundamental forces as vibrations of loops called ‘strings’. Mathematicians often model such strings as loops moving from one contact space to another within a symplectic space.

Resonating with equations

Tsimerman’s passion for solving mathematical puzzles began to show as early as age three, he says. “I had an interest in math as early as I can remember,” says Tsimerman, who is 38 and a Russian-born Canadian at the University of Toronto, Canada.

His main focus is on the theory of numbers, in particular whether, and how, certain equations — ‘Diophantine equations’ — can have whole numbers as solutions. Such equations are intimately connected to algebraic geometry, including to the structure of objects with more than three dimensions, called Shimura varieties. (These also play an essential part in the proof of Fermat’s last theorem, one of the most celebrated maths breakthroughs of the late twentieth century.)

One of Tsimerman’s favourite facts is that if Shimura varieties could vibrate like physical objects, each of their resonant frequencies would correspond to a specific Diophantine equation. Tsimerman and his collaborators proved one of the central statements in the theory of Shimura varieties in 20215, called the André–Oort conjecture after the two mathematicians who formulated it, and another major conjecture stated by Phillip Griffiths at the Institute for Advanced Study in Princeton6.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the awarding of the prestigious 2026 Fields Medal to four mathematicians, highlighting significant breakthroughs in mathematics with broad implications for science and technology. It emphasizes historic achievements such as the first Chinese-born winners since 1982 and only the third female winner ever, showcasing progress in diversity and excellence in a critical scientific discipline. The detailed description of their contributions to longstanding mathematical problems and their potential impact on understanding physical phenomena demonstrates a meaningful, inspiring advancement in knowledge with wide societal relevance.
articles.personality_title: Four mathematicians win 2026 Fields Medal for groundbreaking discoveries
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Fields Medal is a prestigious award given every four years to up to four mathematicians under 40 years old. It honors major achievements in mathematics and is often called the 'Nobel Prize of Math.' This year’s winners were announced at a big meeting in Philadelphia.

**What happened** – Four mathematicians—Yu Deng, John Pardon, Jacob Tsimerman, and Hong Wang—won the 2026 Fields Medal. Deng and Wang were born in China, making them the first Chinese-born winners since 1982. Wang is also only the third woman ever to win this medal. Each winner works in different areas of math, like fluid behavior, geometry, and number theory, solving problems that have challenged mathematicians for decades.

**Impact** – This award highlights important progress in math, including breakthroughs that help explain how fluids move and how complex shapes behave. For example, Deng proved how tiny particles bouncing around create the flow of fluids we see every day. Pardon solved puzzles related to the geometry of loops and spaces linked to physics ideas like string theory. These discoveries deepen our understanding of the natural world and may influence future science and technology. The diversity of winners also reflects growing inclusion in a field once dominated by a few.

**What's next step** – The winners will continue their research, inspiring other mathematicians and students around the world. Their work may lead to new clues in physics, computer science, and engineering. The Fields Medal winners also often become leaders who help guide future breakthroughs in math.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Four young mathematicians received the 2026 Fields Medal for solving tough math problems that connect to the real world and represent new milestones in diversity and discovery.
articles.personality_title_fr: Quatre mathématiciens récompensés par la médaille Fields 2026 pour des découvertes majeures
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La médaille Fields est une récompense prestigieuse décernée tous les quatre ans à un maximum de quatre mathématiciens de moins de 40 ans. Elle honore des avancées majeures en mathématiques et est souvent appelée le « prix Nobel des mathématiques ». Les lauréats de cette année ont été annoncés lors d’un grand congrès à Philadelphie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Quatre mathématiciens—Yu Deng, John Pardon, Jacob Tsimerman et Hong Wang—ont reçu la médaille Fields 2026. Deng et Wang sont nés en Chine, devenant les premiers lauréats chinois depuis 1982. Wang est aussi seulement la troisième femme à recevoir cette médaille. Chaque lauréat travaille dans des domaines différents des mathématiques, comme le comportement des fluides, la géométrie et la théorie des nombres, résolvant des problèmes qui défiaient les mathématiciens depuis des décennies.

**Impact** – Cette récompense souligne des progrès importants en mathématiques, notamment des découvertes qui aident à expliquer comment les fluides bougent et comment se comportent des formes complexes. Par exemple, Deng a prouvé comment les petites particules en mouvement créent l’écoulement des fluides que nous observons. Pardon a résolu des énigmes liées à la géométrie des boucles et des espaces, en lien avec des idées physiques comme la théorie des cordes. Ces découvertes approfondissent notre compréhension du monde naturel et pourraient influencer la science et la technologie futures. La diversité des lauréats reflète aussi une inclusion grandissante dans un domaine autrefois dominé par peu.

**Prochaine étape** – Les lauréats poursuivront leurs recherches, inspirant d’autres mathématiciens et étudiants dans le monde. Leur travail pourrait ouvrir de nouvelles pistes en physique, informatique et ingénierie. Les médaillés Fields deviennent souvent des leaders qui aident à guider les futures avancées en mathématiques.

**Phrase clé** – Quatre jeunes mathématiciens ont reçu la médaille Fields 2026 pour avoir résolu des problèmes mathématiques complexes liés au monde réel, marquant de nouveaux jalons en diversité et en découvertes.
articles.personality_title_es: Cuatro matemáticos ganan la Medalla Fields 2026 por descubrimientos innovadores
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La Medalla Fields es un premio muy importante que se entrega cada cuatro años a un máximo de cuatro matemáticos menores de 40 años. Reconoce grandes logros en matemáticas y a menudo se le llama el 'Nobel de las matemáticas'. Los ganadores de este año fueron anunciados en una gran reunión en Filadelfia.

**Qué pasó** – Cuatro matemáticos—Yu Deng, John Pardon, Jacob Tsimerman y Hong Wang—ganaron la Medalla Fields 2026. Deng y Wang nacieron en China, siendo los primeros ganadores chinos desde 1982. Wang también es solo la tercera mujer en recibir este premio. Cada ganador trabaja en diferentes áreas de las matemáticas, como el comportamiento de fluidos, la geometría y la teoría de números, resolviendo problemas que habían desafiado a los matemáticos durante décadas.

**Impacto** – Este premio destaca importantes avances en matemáticas, incluyendo descubrimientos que ayudan a explicar cómo se mueven los fluidos y cómo se comportan formas complejas. Por ejemplo, Deng demostró cómo las pequeñas partículas en movimiento crean el flujo de los fluidos que vemos. Pardon resolvió problemas relacionados con la geometría de bucles y espacios vinculados a ideas de la física como la teoría de cuerdas. Estos descubrimientos profundizan nuestra comprensión del mundo natural y pueden influir en la ciencia y tecnología futuras. La diversidad de ganadores también refleja una mayor inclusión en un campo que antes dominaban pocos.

**Próximo paso** – Los ganadores continuarán su investigación, inspirando a otros matemáticos y estudiantes en todo el mundo. Su trabajo puede abrir nuevas pistas en física, informática e ingeniería. Los ganadores de la Medalla Fields suelen convertirse en líderes que guían futuros avances en matemáticas.

**Frase clave** – Cuatro jóvenes matemáticos recibieron la Medalla Fields 2026 por resolver problemas matemáticos difíciles que conectan con el mundo real y marcan nuevos hitos en diversidad y descubrimiento.
articles.image_prompt: A harmonious composition of four glowing mathematical symbols—an elegant fluid wave representing differential equations, intertwined loops symbolizing symplectic and contact geometry, a vibrant lattice structure evoking Shimura varieties, and a delicate string-like vibration weaving through them—all set against a softly glowing abstract background of interconnected nodes and subtle geometric patterns in warm, natural earth tones.

articles.title: Non-epitaxial perovskite polariton laser diode operating under direct current
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 22 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10824-wBy integrating a solution-grown perovskite microplate with chemically inert single-walled carbon nanotube electrodes and embedding them into an optical microcavity, the new microdevices can achieve polariton lasing under a constant direct current at 8 K.
articles.content: Reaching lasing in electrically pumped microdevices based on solution-processed semiconductors poses a substantial scientific and technological challenge. Halide perovskites offer a promising platform for electrical injection1, as their optically excited single-crystal cavities2,3,4 and predesigned5,6 or postprocessed microstructures7,8 have exhibited low lasing threshold. Indirect electrical pumping of a dual-cavity perovskite laser was recently obtained9, using a well-established technological concept of embedding a high-luminosity light-emitting diode (LED) with a high-gain medium into an integrated device10. Direct charge-carrier injection into a perovskite LED excited by auxiliary short, optical pulses resulted in amplified spontaneous emission (ASE)11. Other efforts for rational engineering of architectures12,13,14,15 that allow for high charge-carrier density are still to demonstrate lasing. Here we develop a new strategy for achieving direct electrical pumping of a perovskite laser. We integrate a solution-grown CsPbBr 3 microplate with chemically inert single-walled carbon nanotube (SWCNT) electrodes and embed them into an optical microcavity. By cooling the microdevice down to 8 K at a constant current, a perovskite p–i–n diode is formed that facilitates a balanced carrier injection at high current densities. The perovskite microcavity diode operates in the strong coupling regime, exhibiting polariton lasing under a direct current of 65 μA.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific and technological breakthrough in achieving direct electrical pumping of a perovskite polariton laser diode, which is a major challenge in semiconductor laser technology. This advancement has broad implications for future optoelectronic devices, potentially improving efficiency and enabling new applications. The article provides detailed context on the innovation and its technical significance, indicating a meaningful real-world impact beyond a niche audience.
articles.personality_title: New perovskite laser diode works with direct electric current
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been trying to create tiny lasers that can be powered directly by electricity using special materials called perovskites. These materials are promising because they can be made from solutions and have shown good light properties, but making them lase (produce laser light) using electric current has been very hard.

**What happened** – Researchers combined a perovskite crystal called CsPbBr3 with special electrodes made from carbon nanotubes. They put this setup inside a tiny optical cavity and cooled it to very low temperatures (8 Kelvin, which is extremely cold). When they passed a steady electric current through it, the device produced laser light by a process called polariton lasing.

**Impact** – This is the first time a perovskite laser diode has been made to work directly with electric current in such a simple and effective way. It shows that perovskites can be used to build new types of lasers that might be smaller, cheaper, and more efficient than current devices. This could help develop better lasers for communication, sensors, and other technologies.

**What's next step** – Scientists will likely try to make these lasers work at higher temperatures and improve their designs for practical use. Success in this area could lead to new laser devices that work at room temperature and can be easily manufactured.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Researchers have created a new perovskite laser diode that produces laser light directly from electric current, marking an important step toward advanced, efficient laser technologies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Nouveau diode laser à pérovskite fonctionnant avec un courant électrique direct
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques essaient de créer de petits lasers alimentés directement par l’électricité en utilisant des matériaux spéciaux appelés pérovskites. Ces matériaux sont prometteurs car ils peuvent être fabriqués à partir de solutions et ont de bonnes propriétés lumineuses, mais faire fonctionner un laser avec un courant électrique a été très difficile.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont combiné un cristal de pérovskite nommé CsPbBr3 avec des électrodes spéciales en nanotubes de carbone. Ils ont placé ce montage dans une petite cavité optique et l’ont refroidi à une température très basse (8 Kelvin, très proche du zéro absolu). En faisant passer un courant électrique constant, l’appareil a produit de la lumière laser par un phénomène appelé laser à polaritons.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois qu’un diode laser à pérovskite fonctionne directement avec un courant électrique de façon simple et efficace. Cela montre que les pérovskites peuvent être utilisées pour fabriquer de nouveaux types de lasers plus petits, moins chers et plus efficaces. Cela pourrait améliorer les lasers pour la communication, les capteurs et d’autres technologies.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont essayer de faire fonctionner ces lasers à des températures plus élevées et d’améliorer leur conception pour un usage pratique. Cela pourrait aboutir à des lasers fonctionnant à température ambiante et faciles à fabriquer.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des chercheurs ont créé un nouveau diode laser à pérovskite qui émet de la lumière laser directement grâce à un courant électrique, une avancée importante pour les technologies laser efficaces.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo diodo láser de perovskita que funciona con corriente eléctrica directa
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han intentado crear pequeños láseres que se puedan alimentar directamente con electricidad usando materiales especiales llamados perovskitas. Estos materiales son prometedores porque se pueden fabricar a partir de soluciones y tienen buenas propiedades de luz, pero lograr que funcionen como láser con corriente eléctrica ha sido muy difícil.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores combinaron un cristal de perovskita llamado CsPbBr3 con electrodos especiales hechos de nanotubos de carbono. Colocaron este sistema dentro de una pequeña cavidad óptica y lo enfriaron a una temperatura muy baja (8 Kelvin, muy cerca del cero absoluto). Al pasar una corriente eléctrica constante, el dispositivo produjo luz láser mediante un proceso llamado láser de polaritones.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que un diodo láser de perovskita funciona directamente con corriente eléctrica de forma sencilla y efectiva. Esto muestra que las perovskitas pueden usarse para crear nuevos tipos de láseres más pequeños, baratos y eficientes. Esto podría mejorar los láseres para comunicaciones, sensores y otras tecnologías.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos intentarán que estos láseres funcionen a temperaturas más altas y mejorarán el diseño para su uso práctico. Esto podría llevar a láseres que funcionen a temperatura ambiente y sean fáciles de fabricar.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Investigadores han creado un nuevo diodo láser de perovskita que emite luz láser directamente con corriente eléctrica, un paso importante hacia tecnologías láser avanzadas y eficientes.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing crystalline microplate made of translucent green and yellow hues, delicately embedded between slender, dark carbon nanotube strands shaped like fine threads, all enclosed within a softly illuminated optical cavity resembling a transparent geometric prism, with gentle currents of light flowing through the structure symbolizing balanced electrical charge injection and polariton lasing at low temperature.

articles.title: Scientists discover the hidden brain switch that assigns bees their jobs
articles.summary: Researchers found that specific brain circuits help decide which jobs bees perform as they age. When those circuits were switched off, older worker bees began caring for the queen again instead of carrying out their normal later-life duties. The findings reveal that a bee colony’s complex division of labor may be guided by neural switches hidden inside each bee’s brain.
articles.content:
Scientists have identified brain circuits that help determine which jobs worker bees perform inside a colony. Biologists from Heinrich Heine University Düsseldorf (HHU) and the universities in Cologne and Frankfurt/Main found that they could change bee behavior by manipulating a particular gene and selectively reducing activity in certain neural circuits.

The findings, published in the scientific journal Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS), offer new insight into how a colony organizes its workforce without a leader assigning tasks.

How Bees Divide Work Without a Leader

Any successful community depends on its members completing the right jobs at the right time. Humans can discuss responsibilities and deliberately assign them, but a bee colony has no central planner. Even so, bees (Apis mellifera) divide their work with remarkable efficiency.

A worker bee's responsibilities typically change as it grows older. Young workers care for the queen and developing larvae. Later, they help build and maintain the hive and defend it against threats. Only during the final stage of their lives do they leave the colony to search for food.

These behavioral changes are directed by interactions among roughly one million neurons in the bee brain. Until now, however, scientists did not understand how the nervous system produces this age-dependent progression from one task to another.

A Gene Linked to Worker Bee Behavior

Important clues emerged from earlier research by a team at HHU led by Professor Dr. Martin Beye of the Institute of Evolutionary Genetics. The scientists were studying a gene known as doublesex when they observed an unusual behavioral change.

When the gene was deactivated in older worker bees, the insects began caring for the queen again. This responsibility is normally performed only by younger bees, suggesting that doublesex plays an important role in controlling age-related work behavior.

Because the doublesex gene operates only within certain neural circuits, the discovery gave Professor Beye's team, together with researchers from the universities in Cologne and Frankfurt/Main, a way to investigate how specific parts of the bee brain influence social behavior.

Scientists Switch Off Specific Neural Circuits

The researchers selectively silenced neurons associated with doublesex. To accomplish this, they used the gene to produce a protein that suppresses neural activity. They then activated that protein by feeding the bees a particular substance, allowing them to reduce activity only in the targeted circuits.

Once those circuits were inhibited, older workers returned to caring for the queen instead of performing the duties expected at their age.

Dr. Jana Seiler, lead author of the PNAS study, said, "The older worker bees then resumed caring for the queen, which only younger bees would do otherwise. When the circuits were not inhibited, the bees exhibited their normal, age-dependent behavior. In this way, we were able to control which tasks the worker bees performed."

Neural Communication May Organize the Hive

The results indicate that task organization within a bee colony has a neural foundation. When activity in particular areas of the brain is reduced, other neural circuits may become active and trigger a different set of behaviors.

The findings provide early evidence that communication between neural circuits helps determine whether a worker bee nurses the queen, builds or guards the hive, or searches for food.

Professor Beye concludes, "The ability to control the social behavior of bees offers us new opportunities to explore the fundamentals of innate behavioral diversity and social cooperation. The solution to the secret of how bees and other animals cooperate so well without a blueprint for work is likely hidden in the brain's neural circuits."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery revealing how neural circuits in bees' brains control their division of labor, providing meaningful insights into social behavior and cooperation in animal societies. This breakthrough has broad implications for understanding innate behavioral diversity and social organization, which is inspiring and impactful beyond a niche audience.
articles.personality_title: Scientists uncover brain circuits that control bees’ jobs
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Bees live in colonies where different worker bees do different jobs as they grow older. Young bees care for the queen and baby bees, while older bees build the hive, guard it, or collect food. Scientists wanted to understand how bees know which tasks to do without a leader telling them.

**What happened** – Researchers from German universities discovered that certain brain circuits in bees decide what jobs they perform. By turning off a gene called doublesex in older bees, they made those bees start caring for the queen again, a job usually done only by younger bees. They did this by using a special protein to silence specific neurons, changing the bees’ behavior.

**Impact** – This shows that a bee’s brain controls its work through neural switches. It helps explain how bees organize their colony’s work so well without any boss giving orders. The discovery gives new clues about how animals cooperate and divide tasks naturally.

**What’s next step** – Scientists plan to study how these brain circuits communicate with each other to control behavior. This could reveal more about how social cooperation works in bees and other animals.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists found brain circuits that act like switches to control which jobs bees do inside their colony.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent les circuits cérébraux qui contrôlent le travail des abeilles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans une colonie d’abeilles, les ouvrières changent de tâches en grandissant : les jeunes s’occupent de la reine et des larves, tandis que les plus âgées construisent la ruche, la protègent ou cherchent de la nourriture. Les scientifiques voulaient comprendre comment les abeilles savent quoi faire sans chef pour leur dire.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs d’universités allemandes ont découvert que certains circuits cérébraux déterminent les tâches des abeilles. En désactivant un gène appelé doublesex chez les abeilles plus âgées, ils ont fait en sorte que ces abeilles s’occupent à nouveau de la reine, un travail normalement réservé aux plus jeunes. Ils ont utilisé une protéine spéciale pour bloquer certains neurones et changer le comportement des abeilles.

**Impact** – Cela montre que le cerveau des abeilles contrôle leur travail grâce à des interrupteurs neuronaux. Cela explique comment elles organisent si bien le travail dans la colonie sans chef. Cette découverte apporte de nouvelles pistes pour comprendre la coopération et la division des tâches chez les animaux.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont étudier comment ces circuits cérébraux communiquent entre eux pour contrôler les comportements. Cela pourrait révéler plus sur la coopération sociale chez les abeilles et d’autres animaux.

**Une phrase clé** – Des scientifiques ont trouvé des circuits cérébraux qui agissent comme des interrupteurs pour contrôler les tâches des abeilles dans leur colonie.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren circuitos cerebrales que controlan el trabajo de las abejas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En una colonia de abejas, las obreras hacen diferentes trabajos a medida que envejecen: las jóvenes cuidan a la reina y a las crías, mientras que las mayores construyen la colmena, la protegen o buscan comida. Los científicos querían entender cómo las abejas saben qué hacer sin un líder que les diga.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de universidades alemanas descubrieron que ciertos circuitos cerebrales deciden qué trabajos hacen las abejas. Al apagar un gen llamado doublesex en abejas mayores, hicieron que estas abejas volvieran a cuidar a la reina, un trabajo que normalmente hacen solo las jóvenes. Usaron una proteína especial para silenciar ciertas neuronas y cambiar el comportamiento de las abejas.

**Impacto** – Esto muestra que el cerebro de las abejas controla su trabajo mediante interruptores neuronales. Ayuda a explicar cómo las abejas organizan tan bien el trabajo en la colonia sin un jefe. El descubrimiento ofrece nuevas ideas sobre cómo los animales cooperan y dividen tareas naturalmente.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean estudiar cómo se comunican estos circuitos cerebrales para controlar el comportamiento. Esto podría revelar más sobre la cooperación social en abejas y otros animales.

**Una frase para recordar** – Los científicos encontraron circuitos cerebrales que funcionan como interruptores para controlar qué trabajos hacen las abejas en su colonia.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a honeybee colony inside a softly glowing hive, with stylized neural circuits subtly intertwined around and within the bees, symbolizing brain activity; one prominent bee gently tending a glowing queen bee, while faint, flowing lines connect different bees to represent shifting roles and communication within the colony, all rendered in natural, earthy tones.
